speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 23rd of July, 1995. And we are in Sydney, Australia. OK. Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What happened then, John? You, you slept in the barn?

speaker: interviewee
text: First night free.

speaker: interviewer
text: First free night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Slept on the haystack one night. Next day, we found other people from the same camp, which we knew in the village. And they said, come to us. We said, what do you mean to us? Where are you? They says, we are living in a castle. I says, how far away? He says, about a kilometer out of town in, in the bush setting, rocks around, mountains started there. Still, it was snowing 1st of May in southern Germany. That day, it was snowing. And I said, who's castle? What castle? He said, you'll see. So we went there. There were about 15 in the castle. It was-- I think it-- her name was [? Krauss-Maffei. ?] That was a widow who, who was a shareholder in one of the aircraft factories. And it was the summer or winter palace. You could call it either. So, they were lavish rooms with, with chandeliers and, and French furniture.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was nobody living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody living there. Just the caretaker, who was a elderly German, and his, his wife. So we came down. And it, it's one, one of the group was a butcher from profession. And we said, what we cooking for dinner tonight? And he said, here is a place with chickens. Each chicken had a tag with a name and, and how old it is. And it was like an experimental thing. So we got some chickens. We killed it. And we made chicken soup for, for dinner. And we saw in-- we saw a BMW, huge car, and, and two motorbikes, no, no tires, all hidden. We went to the car. We couldn't even drive. So-- but we were looking-- in the basement, we found carrots buried in sand. And as I undone the carrots, I, I, I found a box, a German pistol, completely dismantled, in grease, filled up in grease. And we brought this out. And we found a few things. I found a hidden Blaupunkt shortwave receiver, and also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a draftsman set, Richter chrome set, beautiful set for doing work, drafts-- drafting work. And that's it. We, we had there a few-- we were there about two nights, some-- a very short time, slept on mattresses on the floor. They had about 15 rooms, at least, in that place. It was a two-story place. And the, the caretaker was a very scared man, scared of us. We didn't touch him, nothing. And he lived in separate quarter in the yard. And then we saw next, two days later, Russians arrived, ex-prisoners of war, not from camp, from-- with Russian-- with big yellow paint on their back, Kriegsgefangen, a KGF. Some were already discarded their Russian uniform, prisoner uniform. And they, they dressed up in, in, how do you call it, cylinder hats and dinner jackets--

speaker: interviewer
text: Top hats.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and all sorts of things they could grab in wardrobes in the village. You would think it's a party going somewhere. And they were drunk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were still in your striped clothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in our striped clothing. Yeah, we didn't have any clothing left. It was cold, because it was snowing that May. But it was not already-- not winter. And these Russians tell us to clear out of the place, because they are going to burn it to the ground. We, we were scared. They-- a whole truckload of them. There could have been 25 or 30 of them. And they had-- some of them had weapons. They came in and, you see, they started breaking the furniture, took one of those expensive chairs, hit, hit it against things with the leg, throwing the crystal chandeliers. Everything was rubble. Carpets, they put petrol, ignited it. It was all on fire in about 20 minutes, just burned down. Then, yeah, they took the caretaker, started beating him up. They wanted to know where the tires are for the car. So finally, he told them where the tires were. They had three rows of cut wood for firewood cut in small bits from the cut logs packed up to the ceiling for next winter, what we didn't use up this winter yet. The war got too close. And behind, the tires, there were the tires. So they took the car and the tires.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as they burned this place down, the Americans arrived. And they-- we were on the side of the road. There was a main road going about a few hundred yards from there. And one American stopped and said, how many are you? Says, so many. Board the truck with benches. How many were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we were about 15 to 20 people. Now, I didn't count the Russians. The Russians had their own truck. And they disappeared. The fire went-- the Americans had to put it out, because the Germans didn't have the equipment. And they were a day before-- or after liberation. But before they can come and do it, they couldn't do nothing. It's just burned out shell. And they-- the Americans took us to Landsberg, Landsberg am Lech, which was about 40 kilometers from there, and housed us in a-- in a huge camp. It was an artillery school of the German army. And they had blocks of-- three-floor blocks, large blocks. Each block was named after a German city. Weener Haus, Stuttgart Haus, and, and so on. And huge kennels were like driveways under the building, where they learned to count and to measure distances and all that. They were really old ones, not not up-to-date. They used them up on the front. You would see they were-- they were pieces good for, for putting in a pack for, for souvenir, huge pieces. And we were there. We had-- we had the guard in the American office. And we found people from the camp, from other camps. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I told these two guys who were with me from my place, we're-- we have to get-- we're getting food here. But not, not-- we need some better food, if we can get it. Let's do some work. So, we go out to Landsberg, in the city. That's about 30,000, 40,000 people. It's a small town. It has a, a fortress where Hitler got, got incarcerated before he took power in Germany. That's now a holiday-- holiday say I-- not a holiday, it's a tourist visiting place. I never went to see. I don't think even it was open so early for the public. But we saw the building. It's like a huge-- like Long Bay Jail, only bigger, the building. He had one room where he was, I don't how many months, where he was put in by the Kaiser. And we went from military command to military command, if there is work. I was working in the kitchens. We come across-- because this, this-- the American army requisitioned German restaurants stock, lock, and barrel. They used only the tables and some chinaware for the officers. The soldiers had their, their metal cups and saucers and knives and spoons all on the handle. So there was a unit there with mainly African Americans. They were drivers of heavy trucks. And they said, you go up to the first floor. The sergeant will tell you if they need you. And it was broken English, broken German. And you start to understand a bit. So we saw the sergeant up there, and laying on his blanket, dark, very black, like a pair of boots, and with two German girls massaging his back. He was a really fat fellow. And then he was chewing gum and telling us in English, but with a Yankee, southern accent. I didn't understand much what he said, but I saw that he was not willing. And what he said, really, was he only employees women, not men, in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So off we went. And we found a kitchen. But there was one room, so I got the job, because I was small-- the smallest one. And they needed somebody who could enter a little cement enclosure through a small hole where they kept ice that they make. They bring ice from the ice factory. And every three, four days, they bring a utility of ice. And they-- you have to pack it in with gloves and everything. So I got the job. It was a good job in the kitchen. And your friends left. They--

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked during the day in the kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they-- and you separated now from your friends.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went back at night to sleep in the-- in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the same town. But I could take food with me. They didn't pay, the Americans. They gave me-- they told me in advance, you get cigarette rations, five packets of cigarettes, whatever you like-- Raleigh, Chesterfield, Camel, Lucky Strike. Or-- and also, they gave me Prince Albert tobacco, one box, a metal box, and cigar-- a box of cigar. I didn't smoke. So the Germans didn't have cigarettes. You could buy anything for cigarettes those days, because when an American soldier throw the bit away, there was always a German kid jumping on it, extinguishing it, and take it to his father back home, no tobacco. Then they started sending their kids to the military kitchen to take the leftovers and, and dishes home after the meal. There were a hundred soldiers, 20 officers in this place. And I could only do helping, because only, only military personnel did the cooking. And whatever they opened for lunch or dinner, they didn't give it next day. They throw it out, or we can take it. So, we took what we want. The German kids got the rest of it. And that's it. And I had one problem in, in this place. One day, they checked the teeth of the soldiers. And they said, you work here. You stay in queue. You are checked, too. So American dentist was there in uniform. And he took my name. He says, you have a bad tooth. Come here in here. Next week, one day, he made me an appointment. And I came, and the sergeant told me, you must go, because if you don't go, you can't work here in the kitchen. We all have to go. As the soldiers stood up in queue, They checked their teeth with a mirror and a torch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why, why didn't you want to go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never been in a dentist's place--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ever?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in my life, ever. And I was scared of it. So, what I did, I had to go. So I went to the dentist. And when my turn came up, and he gave me the injection and sent me out for five minutes until it takes effect, I took off. But he already signed my paper, that I attended the dentistry. And I came back happily. I had my face numbed for half a day. I came back and I showed the sergeants, was OK. And I came back with that bad tooth to Romania. I had it extracted maybe a year after. I was scared of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you-- how long were you in this camp, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was there till I got repatriated to Czechoslovakia in a funny way. The Americans had Pilsen under American occupation, which was later given back to, to the Czechs. But the Czechs only got-- for that matter, all groups of Europeans got American transport, provided there are 100 persons wanting the transport. If not, no, you have to wait. So, there were 80 Czechs hardly scratched together that they want to go home. Says, all right, you want to come with us, we will give you transport back to Romania. By that time, our town was already Romania. So I says, all right. You-- only thing you have to say-- or keep quiet, or say that you are Czech. The Americans don't test you. We are 100. They can count the heads, 100 people. They give you three trucks, military trucks, take us to Pilsen and Prague. So that's what we did. We, we went under this Czech group. When we came to Prague, we were housed in a hotel by the Czech Red Cross and given pocket money. We went to Konditorei for coffee and cake. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] First time I see German working with a right arm bent cleaning the streets, and Czech people supervising. [CHUCKLES] But we were there about two or three days, two or three nights. I can't remember exactly. And then we were given the time-- what train to take to Brno and, and to Bratislava. In Bratislava, we changed trains to go to Komarom, a Hungarian border town. But I still had-- I had a few goods, which I organized myself. I had German military air force [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how you call, material for air force uniform for officers. I had maybe 25 meters in, in a roll. I had a pack of sauce, food sauce, 50 in a packet. I had two packets, 100, all in, in my rucksack.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we went through the Danube, on the bridge, there was a pontoon bridge. The ordinary bridge was blown up. And the pontoon bridge was Czech on one end, the Russians in the middle, and the Hungarians on the other end. So everything was all right, till we came to the middle. The Russians took what they liked out of the packets. So they took the good material and then the leather stuff. They left me only a few things they didn't want. And then the Hungarians didn't check us. We came into the Hungarian station, said the next train is about three or four hours, the only train which goes to Budapest. And then they said, if I were you, I wouldn't walk too much around, because Russian prisoners of war, German prisoners of war taken by the Russians, are taken to Russia through that station. And if they are missing some runaway, they will catch you and put you in. They can catch us, put us in. They don't care what nationality you are. They are assigned for 60 in a-- in a carriage, they have to report that 60 are here when they come to Russia. So, I said, oh, what? No names, they had only head count. The same like the Germans, the Russians did. So we kept away, not to be seen, because there were Russians there, but not the train. So finally, this Hungarian train arrived. We boarded it. And we moved to Budapest. And we were housed in a school called Elizabeth School, Elizabeth Street School, where we had a shower and accommodation, food. So, we went to have a shower. We saw the shower wall full of names, who passed the shower before us. So we added our names there, too. We got another train after three days, who took us to the Romanian place, to the border town. So, I went back to Oradea. You went back to your hometown. And what did you find?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it took me-- I didn't go to my village for, for a while, maybe three, three, four weeks. I had a rest until I get-- the trains in Romania were still running not right, and no buses. Even the-- they pinched the-- the train windows, the old train windows had leather to pull up with holes to secure in certain positions. But they cut those off for, for repairing shoes, because they were quite heavy. They were about 3/8, 1/4-inch-thick leather. And I said, I'll stabilize a little bit. Then I went out to see the village. Was all open, nobody occupied. And now Romanian gendarmes were living where the Hungarian gendarmes left. And that house was belonging to us. Nobody-- you couldn't sell, because they were in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, it was empty. The village was empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: Ours was empty. But the, the other house was occupied by Romanian gendarmes after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your aunt's house, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Well, I lived in my aunt's place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where I lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went with her to the village, about three weeks later. She came with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: I found that oil painting and, and some photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of your-- of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. All furniture, everything was carried away, who knows what. Wasn't worth much anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you remain in Hungary-- in Romania now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was there till I went to Israel, till '47-- 20-- day of-- day of-- Christmas day, day after Christmas, around 25, 26 of December, '47.

speaker: interviewer
text: So for two years, you were in, in, in Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two years and-- must have been two years and two months, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you, you made your way to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went back to school in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in the two years that you were there, you'd gone--

speaker: interviewee
text: I went one year back to school. And one year I worked in, in the lithography.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Printing.

speaker: interviewer
text: The work that your uncle did.

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's lithography.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was nationalized. I just worked there, like a apprentice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So, you spent-- you spent two years after the war in Romania. And then you made your way to Israel on your own.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were on your own. And you went straight to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I went to Cyprus, was captured on the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many-- how long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cypress?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Detained?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Six months in a British camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that all right for you, the British camp? You had food, and you had a roof over your head.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the British gave us raw food. We had to form groups or find a family who will cook it, raw potatoes, raw meat. But I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] but I, I had a good time. I swam during the morning hours and went to weddings. There were all sorts of weddings at night, open weddings, open disused tennis courts, a platform. And they have good bands, all French-speaking Jews from Morocco and Nigeria. They were captured before us. They were already in the camps. And married, married couples, you didn't have to have an invitation. You just came there and eat. But I went to those weddings until I got jaundice from some spoiled fish, herring or something on the sandwiches. And I got transferred to a military hospital in in Nicosia, the capital of Cyprus, British military hospital. I was there about two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when-- when did you arrive in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the first Armistice, when, when was the first Armistice in June. The end of the Armistice day, they allowed people under 17 or over 50 from Cyprus. Now, I was 20, going on 21. And they told me to, to shave, so not-- no beard, divide your hair, and go with shorts, so you'll-- and I was small. You look 17, and we'll make your docket 17. I still have the docket. I can show it to you. Everything was right. But that was a Jewish agency who made the docket. And the officer put in-- my name was, in Hebrew, [HEBREW], which is also male or also female name. So they-- the person who did that didn't know. And they put in sex F. And I didn't even notice. My English was no good. Even the Hebrew wasn't good. So when my turn came to board the ship, I went through the British control out. Two British officers checked my agency document. And, and one was calling over to the other and laughing. He said, this guy has a female docket. And there were three officers from the Jewish agency at the same table. So he gave it-- he called to these agents. He said, what's going on here? So he, he say then, tell me in Yiddish to go back and go to the other gate, which was a couple hundred yards, another outlet, also to the harbor, through, through another British control. So he, he crossed over the F and put an M. So I went to the other gate, so these two officers wouldn't know about it. And I went out.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrived there on 4th of July, '48. Was still Armistice, still, I think, the 10th of July, six weeks-- six days more.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, John, how long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you married there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your children are born-- were the-- your children born in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, born in Sydney.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, so you came to-- when did you come to Australia?

speaker: interviewer
text: With your wife and your-- and your twins.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, with your wife, sorry. And your twins were born here. When were they born? Which year?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: 10 years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is John Ullman. I'm happy to be on this-- what do you call it? Part of a program.

speaker: interviewee
text: Program. And I'd like to introduce my lovely family-- my wife, Shulamit, Anita, and Naomi, twins.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you girls like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm Anita, as Dad said already. Yeah, Dad took a little bit of persuasion to, to do this. We both sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Hassled.

speaker: interviewee
text: --pushed and prodded a little bit. And it was easy to push Mum. So I think Mum, in turn, pushed Dad, so we had a domino effect. [LAUGHS] Because Dad, Dad has some great stories to tell. And sometimes, when we've got friends over, he tells them. But we don't have a tape recorder. And so this way, it's really good for us, so we can get the facts right, because sometimes we don't remember all the details that Dad remembers. So it will be really interesting to see his impressions and that sort of thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because he has so many details of things, it's hard to actually take it all in at once. So if we've got it on record, I think it will be really good. And you know, it's really important for history, I think, especially when people say things like that didn't happen. How can you say that?

speaker: interviewer
text: True.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Shula, ?] would you like to add something?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm actually pleased that John agreed to do the program, because I felt that he has a lot to say. And it had to be said in sequence. And no matter how many times I said, oh, we have to put it on a-- on tape, we never got around to do it. But this one is constructed So I think it's well-- it's good that we did it. Feels like a load off my shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're delighted, because I think it's a wonderful thing to do for your own family and for the world outside. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my mother, which I found in our home, the abandoned house in the village, in 19-- after, after I came back in 1945. It would have been September.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is the photo damaged, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo is damaged because they trampled on, on the-- they trampled on it on the floor. There were markings. People were walking over it. And the whole house was empty, only bits of newspapers and books and some photos.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's your mother's name? Could you just repeat her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name is Lenke, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] born Steinberger. And her Hebrew name is [? Leah. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my auntie, who was two years younger than my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Steinberger. And husband [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was deported in 1944. This is Leiberman, Alex Leiberman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She survived the Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they live, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived in, in Oradea, Nagyvarad in Hungarian language. And I was living in their place during six years I attended secondary school, or high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my auntie, my mother's sister, who also died in Auschwitz with her three-year-old son. And her husband returned after the war and emigrated to Israel, where he died in a [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dr. [? Lengyel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: No, what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Elizabeth ?] [? Bershka. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And her-- what was her married surname? [? Bershka. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lengyel. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Lengyel. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Lengyel ?] surname, born Steinberger.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: I see this photo is also damaged.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that was also trampled on by walking people.

speaker: interviewer
text: One of the photos you found on the floor of your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: In this picture-- a family picture taken in the yard of our house in Alsovalko. From left to right, it's my grandfather, Shlomo Steinberger. Next to him is my grandmother, [? Hannah ?] Steinberger. In my rear is [? Bershka ?] Steinberger. And then my mother, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [? Leah ?] Steinberger, and my father, Shlomo Solomon, and me in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's you in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, very sweet. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture taken in 1945, after I returned to my hometown, where actually I studied in high school, in Oradea. And this is my auntie, who also survived Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is-- well, she married three, three times. She was [? Gisella ?] Leiberman when she got deported. Then she married, in Oradea, Mr. [? Katz, ?] who died of heart attack. And she married in Israel the third time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photo of myself, taken roughly about five months after liberation in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you living at the time, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in-- as soon as I arrived home in Romania.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of a childhood friend who was with me all the time during the Holocaust. He is presently living in Bat Yam in Israel. He's deaf and dumb. So is his wife. But he's got two sons and a daughter OK, perfect.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's his name, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Lazar Pearl.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Immediately after?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, maybe a year after.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my school certificate from 1946, when I finished two years after the war in Oradea, in the state school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a, a receipt of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Germany. They paid me 1,800 mark for loss of freedom, for interned in a camp for a year. They paid 150 mark per month.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what indicates that you were interned? Is there anything on the document that shows that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [GERMAN] means [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] interned or, or jailed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: In prison.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You ready, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: This one is a notification from Germany that they paid 850 mark per person for my parents and myself, three, three persons, confiscated jewelry, Hungarian Auschwitz cases.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you receive this, John?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, 20 years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a oil painting of, of my mother did at age of 15, in 1917, when she attended high school. It's got a mark in the middle of it vertically. It was folded. And a neighbor in the village kept it for me without a frame.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you came back to the village, she--

speaker: interviewee
text: She gave it to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I returned, I, I got it back from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape six of the interview with John Ullman. It's the 23rd of July, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And that's also the end of the whole interview.
